target.name: saframycin A substructure 14
target.motivation: To overcome synthetic limitations of Pictet–Spengler reactions for saframycin synthesis and achieve greater structural diversification, enabling the development of reversible covalent DNA binders with tailored sequence preferences.

success_criteria.free_text: Efficient synthesis of the substructure of saframycins bearing isoquinoline and THIQ units in just four steps from the modular assembly of three components, with regiocontrolled 6-endo hydroamination and temporary protection of nitrile and phenolic hydroxy groups.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond between C11 of THIQ and alkyne
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection allows for the modular assembly of the alkyne, THIQ segment, and benzaldehyde building blocks.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-N and C-O bonds forming the 2,3-diaminobenzofuran ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection simplifies the complex 2,3-diaminobenzofuran moiety to an aldehyde and an aminonitrile precursor, which can be formed in one pot.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-N bond forming the left THIQ ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection allows for the formation of the isoquinoline ring from an internal alkyne and a secondary amine, with regiocontrol.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy aims to overcome the limitations of biomimetic Pictet–Spengler reactions by developing a de novo synthetic process independent of aromatic ring substituents. It involves a streamlined modular assembly of the left THIQ segment through copper(I)-catalyzed three-component coupling, followed by tandem 2,3-diaminobenzofuran formation, and gold(I)-promoted 6-endo cyclization with spontaneous transformations to yield the target substructure. The 2,3-diaminobenzofuran moiety is designed to facilitate regiocontrolled 6-endo cyclization, be thermodynamically stable, and serve as a temporary protecting group for the phenolic hydroxy and nitrile groups.

thought.background: The alkyne segment 8 is a crucial building block for the three-component coupling. It needs to be prepared from a known starting material.
thought.gap: How to efficiently synthesize alkyne 8 from 2-ethynyl-6-hydroxybenzaldehyde (15) with appropriate protecting groups.
thought.decision: To prepare alkyne 8, a known starting material, 2-ethynyl-6-hydroxybenzaldehyde (15), will be subjected to protecting group manipulations in three steps. This is expected to yield the desired alkyne 8.

outcome.observation: Alkyne segment 8 was prepared by protecting group manipulations in three steps from the known starting material, 2-ethynyl-6-hydroxybenzaldehyde (15).

evidence.text_span: The alkyne segment 8 was prepared by protecting group manipulations in three steps from the known starting material, 2-ethynyl-6-hydroxybenzaldehyde (15), which can be readily synthesized from commercially available 1-bromo-3-fluorobenzene (see Scheme 2 and Supporting Information File 1 for details).

thought.background: Alkyne 8, THIQ 9, and benzaldehyde are available. The key strategy involves a copper(I)-catalyzed three-component coupling.
thought.gap: How to achieve regioselective C–C bond formation at the C11 position to form 10.
thought.decision: To form 10, a copper(I)-catalyzed three-component coupling reaction of alkyne 8, THIQ 9, and benzaldehyde will be performed. This is expected to proceed with exquisite control of regioselectivity, involving in situ generation of iminium cations and subsequent nucleophilic attack of a copper acetylide.

outcome.observation: Copper(I)-catalyzed three-component coupling reaction of alkyne 8, THIQ 9, and benzaldehyde, proceeded with exquisite control of regioselectivity to afford 10 in an excellent yield of 92%.

evidence.text_span: Copper(I)-catalyzed three-component coupling reaction of alkyne 8, THIQ 9, and benzaldehyde, proceeded with exquisite control of regioselectivity to afford 10 in an excellent yield of 92% [43-46].

thought.background: Compound 10 contains a cyclic acetal that needs to be removed to expose the aldehyde for the subsequent Strecker reaction.
thought.gap: How to efficiently deprotect the cyclic acetal in 10 to obtain aldehyde 16.
thought.decision: To obtain aldehyde 16, the cyclic acetal in 10 will be removed using TFA in THF/H2O. This is a standard deprotection method for acetals.

outcome.observation: After removal of the cyclic acetal, the structure of 16 was confirmed by serial X-ray crystallography.

evidence.text_span: After removal of the cyclic acetal, the structure of 16 was confirmed by serial X-ray crystallography using an X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL) [deposition number CCDC 2352718) [54,55].

thought.background: Aldehyde 16 is available. The next step is to construct an α-aminonitrile 17 and then form the 2,3-diaminobenzofuran 11 via intramolecular cyclization.
thought.gap: How to perform the Strecker-type reaction and subsequent cyclization in a one-pot sequence to obtain 11 efficiently.
thought.decision: To obtain the key intermediate 2,3-diaminobenzofuran 11, a Strecker-type reaction on aldehyde 16 will be performed using acetone cyanohydrin and a catalytic amount of triethylamine in dichloromethane at 15 °C. This is expected to proceed in one-pot via generation of aminonitrile 17 and subsequent nucleophilic attack of the phenolic hydroxy group.

outcome.observation: The key intermediate, 2,3-diaminobenzofuran 11, was obtained in one-pot, presumably via generation of the aminonitrile 17 and subsequent nucleophilic attack of the phenolic hydroxy group to form the five-membered ring. The best results afforded 11 in 73% isolated yield.

evidence.text_span: Our efforts to optimize this one-pot sequence led to the best results, affording 11 in 73% isolated yield, when acetone cyanohydrin and a catalytic amount of triethylamine were used in dichloromethane with careful control of the reaction temperature at 15 °C (Table S2, Supporting Information File 1).

thought.background: The 2,3-diaminobenzofuran 11 is in hand. The next step is to construct the left isoquinoline ring via gold(I)-mediated 6-endo hydroamination.
thought.gap: How to selectively promote the desired 6-endo cyclization (11 to 12) over the competing 5-exo pathway (leading to 13) and what conditions are optimal for this cascade.
thought.decision: To construct the left isoquinoline ring, 11 will be treated with a cationic gold complex (generated in situ from AuCl(PPh3) and AgNTf2) and an excess amount of triethylamine in 1,2-dichloroethane at 65 °C. This is expected to result in regiocontrolled hydroamination, followed by spontaneous dehydrogenative oxidation and ring opening to yield 14.

reaction.named_reaction: gold(I)-mediated 6-endo hydroamination, dehydrogenative oxidation, ring opening

outcome.observation: Treatment of 11 with a cationic gold complex resulted in the intended regiocontrolled hydroamination. The resulting 6-endo cyclization product 12 could not be isolated under these conditions, and instead, the unexpected formation of fluorescent transient intermediate 18 was observed. This intermediate further underwent a ring opening to afford the tetracyclic 14 in a good yield of 60% from 11.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of 11 with a cationic gold complex, generated in situ from AuCl(PPh3) and AgNTf2 [47-49,56], with an excess amount of triethylamine in 1,2-dichloroethane at 65 °C, resulted in the intended regiocontrolled hydroamination. The resulting 6-endo cyclization product 12 could not be isolated under these conditions, and instead, the unexpected formation of fluorescent transient intermediate 18 was observed. ... Overall, triggered by the gold(I)-promoted 6-endo hydroamination between the 2,3- diaminobenzofuran and the alkyne in 11, dehydrogenative oxidation of the resulting 12 to form a fluorescent intermediate 18, and subsequent ring opening allowed a streamlined one-pot access to the substructure of THIQ alkaloids 14 in a good yield of 60% from 11.

thought.background: During the gold(I)-mediated cascade, a fluorescent transient intermediate 18 was observed.
thought.gap: What are the optical properties of this intermediate?
thought.decision: To characterize the optical properties of 18, its UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation, and emission spectra will be measured in CHCl3.

outcome.observation: The UV–vis spectrum of 18 showed two absorption peaks at 334 nm and around 375 nm. When excited at 375 nm, the emission spectra of 18 displayed a relatively broad peak with a maximum around 490 nm. The excitation spectra of 18, corresponding to an emission at 490 nm, was also recorded. The emission quantum yield was Φfl = 0.07.

evidence.text_span: During the development of this cascade synthesis process, we serendipitously discovered the involvement of a sky-blue fluorescent transient intermediate 18 (Figure 2). We therefore investigated the optical properties of 18 in CHCl3 (c = 100 μM) by measuring its UV–vis absorption spectrum as well as its excitation and emission spectra. The UV–vis spectrum of 18 showed two absorption peaks at 334 nm and around 375 nm (gray solid line). When excited at 375 nm, the emission spectra of 18 displayed a relatively broad peak with a maximum around 490 nm (blue solid line). The excitation spectra of 18 (blue dashed line), corresponding to an emission at 490 nm, was also recorded (Figure S3, Supporting Information File 1 for details). Despite the modest emission quantum yield Φfl = 0.07, excited at 375 nm), the chromophore of the pentacyclic intermediate 18 suggests a potential as a fluorescent probe.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the four-step synthesis of 14 from the modular assembly of three components.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of synthetic steps from the modular assembly of three components to the final substructure 14.

validation.significance: This synthetic approach provides a streamlined and modular route to the saframycin substructure, overcoming limitations of traditional Pictet–Spengler reactions. It offers greater flexibility for structural diversification, which is crucial for developing new DNA-alkylating agents. The temporary protection strategy and the discovery of a fluorescent intermediate add novel dimensions to the field.

source.title: Streamlined modular synthesis of saframycin substructure via copper-catalyzed three-component assembly and gold-promoted 6-endo cyclization.
source.authors: Kanno A, Tanifuji R, Yoshida S, Sato S, Maki-Yonekura S, Takaba K, Kang J, Tono K, Yonekura K, Oguri H.
